item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The first [DATA EXPUNGED] seems clumsy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Still not liking the rewrite. Letting the guy go was out of character for the Foundation, right. But the rewrite now feels like a petty potshot at this kind of sentimentalism. Could he not have stayed in confinement instead? What I like in the early SCPs is that some items are simply weird, without necessarily being world threatening monstrosities.

item.title: Staff Post - Open
item.content:encoded: The image that was used in this article could not be found under any source as cc-compliant. Thus, it is assumed that the image is non-compliant with the SCP wiki's license (CC BY-SA 3.0) and has been removed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
What really got me was the "[DATA EXPUNGED]ilar to…" in the second to last paragraph. It's just silly to expunge data only up to the middle of a word. The way I see it, Sweet Idea SCP -> Bland SCP (for the time), so Bland SCP -> Creepy by Lack of Info SCP, but since the rewrite we've developed higher standards for what exactly can be achieved with redactions and lack of info.

-1, but no disrespect.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I just stumbled across this one, and precedent aside, I'm really not a fan. The redactions are heavy-handed and clumsy and the concept doesn't hold up. I think if this were a new article, it would probably not survive.

That said, I'm aware of the history involved here, and I know the piece is a decade old from its original incarnation. So it's tricky.

item.title: This is me
item.content:encoded: This is liteally just me. exept i do eat but i litearlly am never hungry or want food

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I quote the others.
Even for an old article, this is incosistent with the setting.

The Foundation kept 105 under control but not this guy?
Absolutely not. It's even close to 0, being currently at +38.
38 are not THAT MANY votes, and I am quite sure that if this was brought to the attention of many users of the sites, this would go down quickly.

Not advocating for a deletion of the article, just saying that this isn't an example of "We don't do X now, but X worked at the time".
Superheavy redaction was never good imo, and breaking the rules of the Foundation Is ridicolous.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I always hated this entry, it shows nothing but the foundation's lack of integrity and is generally ridiculous

Releasing a humanoid scp allowing for it to find its way back to a Foundation medical facility is questionable

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: -1 from me. The core concept is neat but there doesn’t seem to be much to it beyond that.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I wrote the original article on the old website before it was migrated over. I still check it! That being said, I'd be extremely hesitant to retcon anything or change much, just in the interest of consistency.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This article was written back in 2009. No one's going to come back and "fill the details in".

Also, the "Don't redact containment procedures" is a common and misleading comment that people make. It all comes down to execution.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Too much redacted. Fill the details in, it will make it more disturbing that way. Also, you're not suppose to redact the containment procedures.

item.title: Re: -1, but has potential
item.content:encoded:
Perhaps, but if that was the case, what about the events described in this article would warrant such a sweeping, inflexible response? The regulations around employing and releasing scips are seemingly VERY rigid, and I see nothing about this one that would call for that degree of a reaction.
Then again, depending on what's behind the expunged sections, I suppose it's possible, but that then leads into my other point.

item.title: Re: -1, but has potential
item.content:encoded:
From all I've read, the foundation rarely releases SCP objects, (even safe, sentient ones) and I find it impossible to believe that they would do so without at least administering amnestics to prevent the subject from leaking information about the foundation.

You know, I actually thought this is the whole point of this object.

I mean, what if this scip is the reason why the Foundation does not release any object?

It's like how 076 is the reason the Foundation doesn't employ scips anymore.

item.title: -1, but has potential
item.content:encoded:
Downvoted for two reasons.

Though the idea of an anomaly that provides sustenance is highly intriguing, the potential is overshadowed by the blaring security breach.
From all I've read, the foundation rarely releases SCP objects, (even safe, sentient ones) and I find it impossible to believe that they would do so without at least administering amnestics to prevent the subject from leaking information about the foundation. Perhaps, instead of having him be released, have him kept under loose containment procedures. He stays contained for an extended period of time, and one day, guards find him vomiting blood, incurring the events of the second addendum.

The other main reason is the sheer amount of information that is expunged.
When used sparingly, redacting or expunging information serves to make the reader more curious, prompting them to use their imagination to try and fill in the blanks. However, here it feels like these tools have been used to such an extent that the lack of information leaves the article bare-bones and frustrating.
What I mean by that is that when reading the article, I find myself wanting answers. Yet, the sheer number of questions, scarcity of information, and importance of expunged content makes me feel like it's an exersize in futility to try and work out anything about it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Not really a fan, but that picture scares me. What the fuck is going on? Is that a centipede crawling on a Pacman thing in a bubblebath?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've come back, and have decided I like this. Scips like this need a good twist, and if that has to be done in the form of body horror, then so be it.

I do agree with the removal of the expungement in the containment procedures though. That needs to be gotten rid of, or at least changed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Staff Notice: this user has been permanently banned for trolling.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It is to be kept alive with regular [DATA EXPUNGED]

"Hey Bob, what am I supposed to do here?"

"…huh, it's redacted. Well shit, guess 103's dead then."

-1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm in agreement with Snowskeeper on this one. I like how QSKSw's suggestion works in keeping the lore without really modifying the SCP concept. The Foundation tried to do the right thing but it didn't take. If it was me I'd have had it be something where he was making other people sick, like them not getting nutrients from food they eat around him or something, to avoid the body horror stuff that's become pretty common but I don't really mind that aspect.

But what really chafes me is the containment measures redaction. One of the first things I read before joining was Eskobar's data redaction guide and since then I've gained a huge dislike for redactions in containment procedures. General, medical terms can be used in the place of boring science stuff. Otherwise it's basically like telling the staff that they have to keep this thing alive but they're not allowed to know how to do it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The expungement is a little excessive, and this is generally pretty boring. The concept is pretty cool, though, perhaps it would be better if this were set in the earlier days of the Foundation, and it maybe had more information on the creature in his stomach that seems to be the real anomaly.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree, this would be a significant improvement. As it stands, there is still too much redacted/expunged.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: There are a few spelling and grammar issues, and a bit too much expungement.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Then don't include boring pseudoscience in the containment measures. Containment personnel don't need to know the chemical makeup of the stuff they're extracting from -103's stomach; they just have to know that it's the green bits, it needs to be removed every couple of hours, and they shouldn't touch it. Descriptions of the stuff go in the description—or, if you feel that it's unnecessary/too squicky/whatever, in a fake document. Erandeil's suggestion isn't perfect, but it's better than Foundation personnel apparently not being allowed to know how to contain the thing they need to contain.

I'm more upset about the "It is to be kept alive with regular [DATA EXPUNGED]" line, because there you have redacted something vital to the continued survival of the entity they're trying to contain.

I like QSKSw's suggestion re: Foundation Lore, but at present, I have to downvote this; sorry.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I redact boring pseudoscience. Just because chemical breakdowns of, say, an SCP's poop would be a documented thing in-universe does not mean I want to make them up, nor that anyone would find them entertaining to read.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mistborne's comment is also my comment, and reason for downvote. Containment should not ever be redacted in my opinion. If containment is going to be limited, then at least provide an annotation for a higher security clearance level required.

SCP-103 is to be maintained in a medically induced vegetative state to ensure compliance. It is to be kept alive with regular intravenous nutrition, provided using D Class personnel. Several large abdominal incisions have been created and secured with overhead elastic supported hooks in order to make certain that the incisions are not allowed to heal closed, keeping SCP-103's stomach cavity exposed at all times. Class Four Foundation Restricted Medical Kit is to be employed by all personnel interacting with SCP-103.

All excretions/wastes/objects from SCP-103 are to be incinerated.

+ Enter Level 3 Clearance Code
- Close File

At no time should any personnel come into unprotected contact with any object or substance removed from SCP-103. Should personnel/biologic be exposed to any substance removed from SCP-103 their remains are to be classified SCP-103-1 and incinerated with prejudice, avoiding all contact with any other biological substance.

I just made stuff up and shoved it in the holes to give the idea. I have no idea what the original reviser wanted the substances/tapeworms to do, but this implies that it would be bad, and self propagating.

Then like QSKSw said a comment from an O5 along the lines of-

05- ██ This incident was fundamental to creating the Foundation as it is today. When you question modern protocol for dealing with any SCP subject, stop and think of this. Then ask yourself what would have happened had this been in a civilian hospital?

This is an example of how I would change the article, I am not the author or the reviser.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Redaction in Containment kills this for me.

-1

item.title: Re: SCP-103
item.content:encoded:
I see this one is pretty divisive. I used to agree, even for the early early EARLY Foundation, this one was never that great. But recently something clicked.

[+] Here is my Headcanon and why not to hate this guy.
[-] Here is my Headcanon and why not to hate this guy.

103 was one of the first SCP objects (not a stretch being that it literally is) and it wasn't yet policy to decommission SCP objects instead of releasing them. His anomaly was determined to be unreplicable, so he was worthless to the Foundation, but he wasn't dangerous, so they just let him go. I just assume they gave him Amnesiacs. Otherwise that would just be silly.

When the Foundation started getting reports that he was showing signs of stomach illness, and the source of his anomaly was revealed to be some sort of weird magic tapeworm thing, he was re-contained and the current policies were enacted, of not releasing things just because they're Safe, and terminating things if they need to be decommissioned. SCP-103 isn't the exception to "Secure, Contain, Protect." SCP-103 is the reason for it.

The interesting part of this SCP is and always will be that the Foundation let him go. The actual anomaly present here is uninteresting, no offense to the original author and the rewrite author. BUT, it's uninteresting in all of the right ways. I could not imagine my headcanon being compatible with an SCP with an effect other than demon tapeworms.

Oh, and the anomaly was deemed unreplicable because the anomaly was with the parasite, not the host. They couldn't find a trace of anomalous properties with the guy, so they gave up.

Taking this SCP at face value, as written, both times, yes. It isn't very good. I've been a lurker here for at least 4 years, and it took me that long to think of any of this.

Maybe add a note from the O5 at the end, stating that the Foundation's current containment policies were enacted because of this SCP, and that would pretty much fix it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Why always try to rewrite/safe something?

I think it would have been better to just let this thing die gracefully.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Yes, I've been rereading the entries (it's been a while since I last did that) and there's a lot of great new stuff.
I'm glad that's the case!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I get the feeling that whenever an article is unpopular, we just ramp up the gore level and the upvotes appear

Rest assured, that is not the case. Gore for gore's sake is frowned upon greatly. This is just old, from before that standard was in place.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I know the rewriting is old, but now it's pretty bad and generic…
Another monster from other dimension, another human host for something horrible… I get the feeling that whenever an article is unpopular, we just ramp up the gore level and the upvotes appear, which is pretty sad since the best SCPs here aren't particularly disgusting or graphic.
The original was really bland, but at least it showed that not all SCPs are horrible things that need immediate attention and how much the Foundation cared about what was anomalous but not necessarily dangerous.
Downvoted.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This part bug me…

Subject checked into a small, local hospital in Mid-April at approximately 9:30PM.

It's weird that the record has detail about his admission time, but not bother to keep the date. That, and I hate 12 hour clock.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't really like this one. Maybe it's because of the previous style of the article but it feels like it's trying to hard to be grimdark. That, and the [DATA EXPUNGED] in the containment procedures led me to downvote. Still, the picture is interesting/horrifying.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
This is a late reply, but -

Yes. This was a unique SCP where there wasn't anything disgusting or disturbing - it was just a peculiar little anomaly and it ended happily, which - I think - gave the Foundation more character. But then I guess it was decided that it wasn't scary enough,
And that was that. I think it's a pity.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
For the record, I liked the old version by a large margin. For one (and I am not a fan of using this phrase) operational security was maintained. He stayed in the dark and the Foundation spent next to nothing in resources. Quite literally, the entire thing was without complications. For once. Just once.

Now… it's just another body horror.

But hey, vox populi speaks. People like it as it is.

item.title: Re: Doing my best to be nice here...
item.content:encoded: Shame, that.

item.title: Doing my best to be nice here...
item.content:encoded:
You, uh…

You realize this article was rewritten six months ago, and the issues this post addresses haven't existed since then, right?

I regret deeply that you cannot see me rolling my eyes over the internet.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Never mind. Like, ever.

item.title: Re: The Original Idea
item.content:encoded: Apologies if the rewrite put a damper on your story plans, but it was either that or let it be deleted. I'm glad you like it!

item.title: The Original Idea
item.content:encoded:
I made this SCP back when the site was a normal Wiki, had an idea to also make four more SCPs, each a statue with features of a horseman of the apocalypse. When Famine was going to be introduced to SCP-103, they would have canceled the other out and both would have ceased to exist, Leaving three remaining statues.

Famine causing people to become malnourished and die of starvation in seconds
War to cause extreme rage
Pestilence to give the person in close proximity (without protective gear) a number of diseases that would kill them in a matter of hours
And Death to kill them outright.

That WAS the original idea, but life got hectic and i never had time to write it. I was going to write it, i come back here, and it was all rewritten (Very well, might i add) So Kudos to Pooryoric and everyone else who helped out.

Also, for the people going ''hurr durr they let him go'' or saying this isnt SCP Material, i wanted to take a step back from the super scary crap and just make something unusual. An anomaly as opposed to a monster.

Radioactive Zombie summed it up nicely, ''the Foundation also takes in "mundane" anomalies (well, "mundane" compared to ghost clouds, reality warpers, giant lizards, and inter-dimensional hazards).''

Thanks to those who enjoyed it, and those who helped re-write and improve it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Wow. Talk about swinging way the Hell in the other direction with an edit.

I liked it before, I like it better now.

item.title: Re: Wat.
item.content:encoded: I don't mind the body count, but it seems a little silly to keep the guy's stomach cavity open all the time. There has to be a more hygienic way of dealing with the problem.

item.title: Re: Wat.
item.content:encoded: Works fine for me.

item.title: Re: Wat.
item.content:encoded:
Mostly 'cause those people are dicks

Nah, but I think it can be accomplished without the bodycount. Just make it a subverted bad end, 'cause we've got enough things what make you die horribly and [REDACTED]. Don't get me wrong, I like the change, but it probably would fit a brand spanking new SCP.

I'm not fucking with it because I don't trust my own writing skills, at least, not yet.

item.title: Re: Wat.
item.content:encoded:
The general idea was to not only remove the "just letting an SCP object go" bit, but to provide a concrete reason in character for why that would not be allowed to happen again; IE the manifestation of further effects that pose a threat to human lives.

Basically, going in, I had two things I had to accomplish. Retcon or explain away the release, and give enough of a bodycount that release would not be considered an option in the future IC. Like I said when I posted it, it's not subtle. Personally, I don't like it. I hate writing body horror. But it accomplishes what I intended, and it's gone from +4 to +19, which means that at least 15 people feel I did it right.

item.title: Re: Wat.
item.content:encoded:
Parasites that apparently look freaky and make stuff disappear, infesting an otherwise normal person. These are just in the guy's stomach instead of his hands and back, and look like faces instead of eyes. The only significant difference is that these apparently freaked out when investigated and killed some people.

While it's good that you got rid of the whole "he was a nice man so we let him go" aspect, the new stuff feels a bit shoehorned in.

item.title: Re: Wat.
item.content:encoded: …yeah, what the hell?

item.title: Wat.
item.content:encoded: …In no way whatsoever is it anything at all like SCP-213.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Too much like SCP-213.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Ehh.

Seems to just be another thing what kills you and is icky now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I'll never tell.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Thinking a new SCP would be better, but hurr.

Just wondering, what is that? It looks like an octopus buried near someone's nipple.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I had upvoted the original because I actually liked it, complaints about release and all, but I do have to admit this is way better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Rewritten. Removed "herpa dee derp we just let him go", and added a few dead people and some giant tapeworms with human faces. Good stuff.

It's inelegant, but I've fixed it, tone wise. I think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I agree completely. There are a number of terrible things that could happen when someone can just make things disappear without explanation. Downvoted in hopes of a rewrite.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Give it a sample rewrite Yoric, see if it makes it better.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: If this article hits zero I'll rewrite it. I have a few ideas.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I downvoted just because he was released. I agree with everyone who has said that the Foundation would not permit such a threat to its secrecy. Also, experience suggests that just because he seems to be harmless, he is in fact an anomaly and so is a potential threat. There are plenty of examples of things that seemed to be pretty harmless before they [DATA EXPUNGED]

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The article does describe him as polite and cooperative. Maybe he agreed to never say anything about his ability or the Foundation? Maybe he signed a document forbidding him to divulge information, on penalty of swift detainment and termination.
